past_medical_history: Hypertension, well-controlled on amlodipine for 8 years
Benign prostatic hyperplasia diagnosed 3 years ago
Remote history of lumbar disc herniation L4-L5, treated conservatively

medications.frequency: As needed, up to three times daily

family_history: Father died of myocardial infarction at age 72
Mother lived to age 88, developed 'shaking' in her hands in her late 70s but was never formally diagnosed
No family history of dementia or other neurodegenerative diseases

social_history.occupation: Retired postal worker, retired 2 years ago at age 65
social_history.alcohol: Occasional glass of wine with dinner, approximately 2-3 per week
social_history.tobacco: Former smoker, quit 20 years ago, 15 pack-year history
social_history.living_situation: Lives with wife in a single-story home. Wife accompanies him to the visit and provides collateral history.
social_history.exercise: Used to walk 2 miles daily but has reduced to short walks due to balance concerns over the past 6 months
social_history.driving: Still driving but wife reports he has become slower to react and more hesitant at intersections

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Speech is mildly hypophonic with reduced prosody but no dysarthria. Language fluent without paraphasic errors. Naming, repetition, and comprehension intact. Montreal Cognitive Assessment (MoCA) score 27/30 (lost 1 point on visuospatial/executive, 1 on delayed recall, 1 on verbal fluency). Affect is mildly flat but appropriately engaged.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal, round, and reactive to light bilaterally. Extraocular movements full without nystagmus. Reduced blink frequency noted. Facial expression is hypomimic with masked facies. Facial sensation intact bilaterally. Hearing grossly intact. Palate elevates symmetrically. Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius strength 5/5 bilaterally. Tongue midline without fasciculations.
neurological_exam.motor: Tone: Cogwheel rigidity at the right wrist and right elbow, with mild rigidity at the left wrist. Strength is 5/5 throughout all extremities. Bradykinesia is prominent in the right upper extremity with reduced amplitude and speed on finger tapping, hand opening/closing, and pronation-supination. Mild bradykinesia in the left upper extremity. Right hand rest tremor at approximately 4-5 Hz, pill-rolling in character, diminishes with action and re-emerges when the hand is at rest in the lap. Mild postural tremor in the right hand. No tremor observed in the left hand or lower extremities.
neurological_exam.sensory: Light touch, pinprick, proprioception, and vibration intact in all extremities. No cortical sensory deficits.
neurological_exam.reflexes: 2+ and symmetric at biceps, triceps, brachioradialis, patellar, and Achilles tendons bilaterally. Plantar responses flexor bilaterally. No Hoffmann sign.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose testing without dysmetria bilaterally, though movements are slow on the right. Heel-to-shin testing intact. Rapid alternating movements markedly slow and decremental on the right, mildly slow on the left.
neurological_exam.gait: Gait notable for reduced right arm swing, mildly shortened stride length, and slight forward-flexed posture. Mild difficulty with initiation of gait. No festination observed. Turns require 4-5 steps (slightly increased). Pull test reveals one step backward recovery (retropulsion mildly positive). Romberg sign negative.
neurological_exam.additional: Glabellar tap: positive (Myerson sign, fails to habituate after 10 taps). Handwriting sample obtained: micrographia noted with progressive diminution of letter size across a written sentence. Seborrhea noted on the forehead and nasolabial folds.

patient.chief_complaint: Progressive right hand tremor and difficulty with daily tasks including buttoning shirts and writing, worsening over the past two years
patient.history_present_illness:
Mr. Caruso is a 67-year-old right-handed retired postal worker who presents to the neurology clinic referred by his primary care physician for evaluation of a progressive right hand tremor and generalized slowing of movements. His wife, who accompanies him, reports that she first noticed a subtle intermittent tremor in his right hand approximately two and a half years ago while he was sitting watching television. The tremor was initially mild and occurred only at rest, but has gradually become more persistent and noticeable, particularly when he is relaxed or distracted. He reports that the tremor temporarily stops when he deliberately reaches for an object but returns once the hand is resting again.

Over the past 18 months, he has noticed progressive difficulty with fine motor tasks on the right side. Buttoning his shirt has become a daily struggle, and his handwriting has become progressively smaller and more difficult to read. His wife has noticed that he takes much longer to get dressed in the morning than he used to. He also reports that his right arm feels stiff and does not swing naturally when he walks, which his wife corroborated, noting that she first observed this change approximately one year ago.

More recently, over the past six to eight months, he has noticed that his gait has changed. He describes taking shorter steps and occasionally feeling as though his feet are momentarily stuck to the floor when he starts walking, though frank freezing episodes have not been described. His wife has noticed that he has developed a slight forward stoop. He has not fallen but has felt unsteady when turning quickly. He reports that his voice has become softer, and his wife often asks him to speak up during conversations. He has also noticed mild constipation over the past year, which he attributes to reduced physical activity, and some difficulty with his sense of smell that he has not previously mentioned to a physician.

He denies any cognitive complaints, hallucinations, or vivid dreams. His wife notes no significant personality changes. He continues to manage household finances and participates in social activities, though she observes he has become somewhat less enthusiastic about going out. His sleep has been generally adequate, and he denies any acting out of dreams or unusual nocturnal movements.

signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Scattered punctate periventricular and subcortical white matter hyperintensities consistent with mild chronic small vessel disease

findings.location: Bilateral caudate, putamen, and globus pallidus

signal_characteristics.T2: Normal signal intensity without iron deposition abnormalities beyond mild age-related changes
Normal midbrain morphology, no hummingbird sign
signal_characteristics.SWI: No abnormal signal in the substantia nigra beyond mild age-related changes

additional_observations: No vascular parkinsonism pattern (no basal ganglia lacunar infarcts)
No ventriculomegaly out of proportion to sulcal atrophy (no features of NPH)
No cerebellar or pontine atrophy (no features of MSA)
No mass lesions or pathological enhancement after gadolinium administration
Mild chronic small vessel ischemic changes appropriate for age and history of hypertension
mri.impression: Normal brain MRI for age. Mild nonspecific white matter changes consistent with chronic small vessel disease. No structural cause for parkinsonism identified. No features to suggest normal pressure hydrocephalus, multiple system atrophy, or vascular parkinsonism. The MRI findings are compatible with idiopathic Parkinson's disease, which is a clinical diagnosis with characteristically normal structural brain imaging.

differential_by_imaging.likelihood: Low - no basal ganglia infarcts or significant vascular burden

recommended_actions: Clinical correlation with examination findings for parkinsonism
Consider DaTscan if clinical diagnosis uncertain
No MRI follow-up needed unless new symptoms arise

Thyroid_and_Additional.clinical_significance: Normal thyroid function excludes hypothyroidism as a cause of bradykinesia and psychomotor slowing
Normal B12 level excludes subacute combined degeneration as a cause of gait abnormality
Negative syphilis screening excludes neurosyphilis as cause of parkinsonism
Normal ceruloplasmin makes Wilson disease very unlikely (though less relevant in a 67-year-old, included for completeness)

labs.interpretation: Complete blood count and basic metabolic panel are entirely within normal limits. Thyroid function is normal, excluding hypothyroidism as a cause of psychomotor slowing. Vitamin B12 is normal, ruling out deficiency-related neurological symptoms. RPR is non-reactive, excluding neurosyphilis. Ceruloplasmin is normal. There are no metabolic or systemic abnormalities to explain the patient's parkinsonian symptoms.

findings.location: Left posterior putamen (contralateral to clinically more affected right side)

signal_characteristics.uptake_pattern: Asymmetrically reduced tracer uptake in the left posterior putamen with relative preservation of left caudate uptake, producing a 'period' or 'dot' pattern on the left
signal_characteristics.right_striatum: Mildly reduced uptake in the right posterior putamen, less pronounced than the left

additional_observations: DaTscan (Ioflupane I-123 SPECT) performed according to standard protocol
Patient received potassium iodide thyroid blockade prior to imaging
Asymmetry index for putaminal uptake: 0.72 (abnormal, normal >0.85)
output.impression: Abnormal DaTscan demonstrating asymmetrically reduced dopamine transporter uptake in the bilateral putamina, more severe on the left (contralateral to the clinically more affected right side). This pattern is consistent with a neurodegenerative parkinsonian syndrome, most likely idiopathic Parkinson's disease given the clinical context. The findings confirm presynaptic dopaminergic deficit and effectively exclude essential tremor, drug-induced parkinsonism (in the absence of dopaminergic cell loss), and psychogenic parkinsonism.

differential_by_imaging.likelihood: High - asymmetric putaminal loss with rostro-caudal gradient, concordant with clinical asymmetry
Low - DaTscan pattern alone cannot distinguish these from PD, but clinical presentation strongly favors PD
Excluded - DaTscan would be normal in essential tremor

recommended_actions: Clinical correlation confirms idiopathic Parkinson's disease
Monitor for treatment response, which further supports the diagnosis

Levodopa_Challenge_Test.clinical_significance: Moderate motor disability consistent with early-to-moderate Parkinson's disease

Levodopa_Challenge_Test.test: Post-treatment UPDRS-III Motor Score (after carbidopa-levodopa 25/250 mg)
Levodopa_Challenge_Test.clinical_significance: 50% improvement in motor score, indicating a robust response to levodopa
Robust levodopa response strongly supports diagnosis of idiopathic Parkinson's disease and predicts good therapeutic response

output.interpretation: Acute levodopa challenge test demonstrates a 50% improvement in UPDRS-III motor score after administration of carbidopa-levodopa 25/250 mg. The patient showed marked improvement in right hand tremor, bradykinesia, and rigidity within 60 minutes of dosing. This robust response is highly characteristic of idiopathic Parkinson's disease and argues against atypical parkinsonian syndromes such as MSA or PSP, which typically show poor or absent levodopa responsiveness.
abnormal_values_summary: Post-treatment UPDRS-III 14/132 - 50% improvement, robust levodopa response

Autonomic_Testing.test: Head-up tilt table - Systolic BP drop
Autonomic_Testing.clinical_significance: No significant orthostatic hypotension, which would be expected in MSA

Autonomic_Testing.test: Head-up tilt table - Heart rate response

Autonomic_Testing.clinical_significance: Mildly reduced but within normal limits, no length-dependent sudomotor failure

output.interpretation: Autonomic reflex testing demonstrates no significant orthostatic hypotension (systolic BP drop of only 12 mmHg on tilt, well below the 20 mmHg threshold) and appropriate heart rate response. Sudomotor function (QSART) is within normal limits at all sites. These results argue against multiple system atrophy, in which early and severe autonomic failure is a hallmark. The mild constipation and hyposmia reported by the patient are common non-motor features of idiopathic Parkinson's disease and do not require autonomic failure for explanation.

Neuropsychological_Testing.clinical_significance: Low average processing speed, consistent with bradyphrenia seen in Parkinson's disease
Low average executive set-shifting, borderline but not impaired
Below average semantic fluency, mildly reduced, consistent with early frontal-subcortical dysfunction in PD

output.interpretation: Neuropsychological testing reveals overall average cognitive functioning with subtle deficits in processing speed and semantic fluency. This pattern is consistent with the early frontal-subcortical cognitive profile commonly seen in Parkinson's disease. Memory and language functions are preserved. The MoCA score of 27/30 and neuropsychological profile do not meet criteria for Parkinson's disease dementia or mild cognitive impairment at this time, though longitudinal monitoring is recommended.
abnormal_values_summary: Processing Speed Index 88 - low average, consistent with bradyphrenia in PD
Category Fluency (Animals) T-score 38 - below average, consistent with early frontal-subcortical dysfunction

Polysomnography.clinical_significance: Elevated REM without atonia consistent with REM sleep behavior disorder
Mildly elevated but below clinical threshold

output.interpretation: Polysomnography demonstrates REM sleep without atonia (REM without atonia index 18%, significantly above the 5% threshold) with video evidence of limb jerking and vocalization during REM sleep, consistent with REM sleep behavior disorder (RBD). The patient's wife later confirmed that he occasionally makes jerking movements and vocalizations during sleep, which she had not previously considered noteworthy. RBD is a well-established prodromal marker and non-motor feature of synucleinopathies, particularly Parkinson's disease and dementia with Lewy bodies. No significant obstructive sleep apnea. This finding further supports the diagnosis of idiopathic Parkinson's disease.
abnormal_values_summary: REM without Atonia Index 18% - elevated, consistent with REM sleep behavior disorder

interactions: No significant interactions with tamsulosin, though both may cause orthostatic hypotension - monitor blood pressure
contraindications: Avoid concomitant use with non-selective MAO inhibitors (not applicable in this patient)
Caution with dopamine antagonists (antipsychotics, metoclopramide) which would counteract therapeutic effect
warnings: Additive hypotensive effect possible with amlodipine and tamsulosin - counsel patient on orthostatic precautions
Nausea is common in early treatment; take with a small snack but avoid high-protein meals which can reduce absorption
Monitor for impulse control disorders (gambling, hypersexuality, compulsive shopping, binge eating)
Monitor for dyskinesias with long-term use
May cause vivid dreams or hallucinations, particularly in elderly patients
output.formulary_status: On formulary, no prior authorization required
alternatives: Dopamine agonist monotherapy (pramipexole 0.125 mg TID, titrate to 0.5-1.5 mg TID; or ropinirole 0.25 mg TID, titrate to 3-8 mg TID) - suitable for younger patients but higher side effect burden in elderly
Rasagiline 1 mg daily (MAO-B inhibitor) - mild symptomatic benefit as monotherapy for mild symptoms
Amantadine 100 mg BID - modest dopaminergic effect, may help with tremor

output.query: Parkinson's disease initial treatment carbidopa-levodopa dopamine agonist

results.title: Levodopa and the progression of Parkinson's disease (LEAP trial)
results.source: Verschuur et al., N Engl J Med 2019;380(4):315-324
results.summary: The LEAP trial demonstrated that early initiation of levodopa does not accelerate disease progression compared to delayed treatment. No disease-modifying effect was observed.

results.title: PD MED trial: Long-term outcomes of initial treatment with levodopa vs dopamine agonists vs MAO-B inhibitors
results.summary: Levodopa-based therapy provided better patient-rated quality of life and mobility compared to dopamine agonists or MAO-B inhibitors as initial treatment across all age groups, with no increased risk of motor complications at 7 years.

results.title: MDS Evidence-Based Medicine Review: Treatments for motor symptoms of PD
results.summary: Levodopa remains the most effective treatment for motor symptoms of PD. Dopamine agonists are efficacious but associated with more adverse effects including impulse control disorders and daytime somnolence. MAO-B inhibitors provide mild benefit as monotherapy.

output.summary: Current evidence supports levodopa as the most effective initial treatment for motor symptoms of Parkinson's disease across all age groups. The LEAP trial dispelled the concern about levodopa toxicity, and the PD MED trial showed superior quality of life outcomes with levodopa versus dopamine agonists as initial therapy. For a 67-year-old patient with moderate motor disability, carbidopa-levodopa is the recommended first-line therapy. Starting dose is typically 25/100 mg three times daily, titrated as needed.

differential.key_features: Essential tremor is primarily an action/postural tremor, bilateral, without rigidity or bradykinesia. This patient has a classic resting tremor with pill-rolling character, clear asymmetric cogwheel rigidity, prominent bradykinesia, and gait changes that are inconsistent with essential tremor.

Drug-induced parkinsonism is typically symmetric and occurs after exposure to dopamine-blocking agents. This patient is not on any dopamine antagonists (no antipsychotics, no metoclopramide). His current medications (amlodipine, tamsulosin, acetaminophen) do not cause parkinsonism.

MSA is characterized by early prominent autonomic failure, poor levodopa response, and more rapid progression. This patient lacks significant orthostatic hypotension, urinary incontinence beyond BPH-related symptoms, and has a classic asymmetric presentation more consistent with idiopathic PD.

PSP presents with early postural instability and falls, vertical supranuclear gaze palsy, axial more than limb rigidity, and poor levodopa response. This patient has no gaze abnormality, no early falls, and has prominent asymmetric limb findings characteristic of PD.

NPH presents with the classic triad of gait apraxia (magnetic gait), urinary incontinence, and dementia. MRI shows ventriculomegaly out of proportion to sulcal atrophy. This patient's MRI is normal, his cognition is preserved, and his gait pattern is parkinsonian rather than apractic.

optimal_actions.action: Obtain detailed history including onset, progression, and laterality of motor symptoms, as well as non-motor symptoms (constipation, hyposmia, sleep disturbance, mood changes)
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Progressive asymmetric right-sided rest tremor, rigidity, and bradykinesia over 2 years with non-motor features (constipation, hyposmia) supporting synucleinopathy

optimal_actions.action: Perform complete neurological examination with focus on cardinal motor features of parkinsonism and assessment of red flags for atypical parkinsonism
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Asymmetric cogwheel rigidity, resting tremor, bradykinesia predominantly right-sided, reduced arm swing, masked facies, hypophonia, micrographia, no red flags for atypical parkinsonism

optimal_actions.action: Order brain MRI to exclude structural causes of parkinsonism
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal brain MRI for age, no structural lesion, no features of NPH, vascular parkinsonism, or MSA

optimal_actions.action: Order basic laboratory studies including CBC, BMP, thyroid function, vitamin B12, and RPR
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal labs excluding metabolic and systemic causes of parkinsonism

optimal_actions.action: Assess for medication causes of parkinsonism by reviewing medication list
optimal_actions.expected_finding: No dopamine-blocking agents on medication list

optimal_actions.action: Initiate dopaminergic therapy with carbidopa-levodopa as first-line treatment and check for drug interactions
optimal_actions.expected_finding: No significant drug interactions, carbidopa-levodopa 25/100 mg TID as starting dose

optimal_actions.action: Counsel patient and family on diagnosis, disease course, treatment expectations, and safety including fall prevention and driving assessment
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Patient and wife informed about PD prognosis, medication side effects including nausea and impulse control disorders, importance of exercise, and driving safety evaluation

optimal_actions.action: Refer to physical therapy for gait and balance training and occupational therapy for fine motor strategies
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Referrals placed for PT and OT

optimal_actions.action: Consider DaTscan if diagnostic uncertainty exists
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Reduced dopamine transporter uptake confirming presynaptic dopaminergic deficit

optimal_actions.action: Schedule follow-up in 4-6 weeks to assess treatment response and titrate medication
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Follow-up appointment scheduled with plan to assess levodopa response

critical_actions: Perform a thorough neurological examination documenting cardinal parkinsonian features (tremor, rigidity, bradykinesia) and their asymmetry
Order brain MRI to exclude structural and vascular causes of parkinsonism
Obtain basic laboratory studies to rule out metabolic causes (thyroid dysfunction, B12 deficiency)
Review medication list to exclude drug-induced parkinsonism
Initiate appropriate dopaminergic therapy (carbidopa-levodopa preferred for patients over 65)
Counsel patient on disease prognosis, medication side effects, exercise, and safety
contraindicated_actions: Prescribing dopamine-blocking antiemetics (metoclopramide, prochlorperazine) for levodopa-induced nausea - use domperidone or trimethobenzamide instead
Starting a dopamine agonist as monotherapy in a 67-year-old (higher risk of impulse control disorders, hallucinations, and orthostatic hypotension in elderly - levodopa preferred)
Performing lumbar puncture (no indication in this clinical scenario with classic PD presentation)
Prescribing anticholinergics (trihexyphenidyl, benztropine) as first-line therapy in a patient over 65 due to cognitive side effects
Withholding or delaying levodopa therapy based on outdated concerns about levodopa toxicity or 'saving' levodopa for later
Ordering extensive unnecessary workup (brain biopsy, genetic testing) in a straightforward sporadic late-onset PD presentation
key_reasoning_points: The triad of asymmetric resting tremor, cogwheel rigidity, and bradykinesia is the hallmark of idiopathic Parkinson's disease and is present in this patient
The pill-rolling rest tremor that diminishes with action is characteristic of PD and distinguishes it from essential tremor (which is an action/postural tremor)
Non-motor features including hyposmia, constipation, and hypophonia support the diagnosis of a synucleinopathy and are often present before or early in the motor phase of PD
Normal brain MRI is expected in PD and helps exclude structural causes such as NPH, vascular parkinsonism, and features of atypical parkinsonism (cerebellar atrophy in MSA, midbrain atrophy in PSP)
A family history of late-onset tremor in the mother (never formally diagnosed) may represent undiagnosed PD, which is a recognized risk factor
Levodopa is the gold standard initial treatment for patients over 65 with Parkinson's disease, supported by the PD MED trial showing superior quality of life outcomes compared to dopamine agonists
The presence of micrographia is a highly specific finding for PD and reflects the progressive decrement in amplitude (hypokinesia) that is characteristic of the disease

metadata.author: NeuroBench case generator
metadata.estimated_complexity: Straightforward - classic presentation with asymmetric resting tremor, rigidity, bradykinesia, and normal structural imaging
